{{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[image:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|風日祈宮]] | 名称=風日祈宮 | 所在地=三重県伊勢市宇治館町 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命
級長戸辺命]] | 社格=[[皇大神宮]]別宮 | 創建=[[804年]]以前 | 本殿=[[神明造]] | 例祭=風日祈祭 }} '''風日祈宮'''（かざひのみのみや）は[[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町にある[[皇大神宮|内宮（皇大神宮）]]の境内別宮である。 == 概要 == [[image:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|風日祈宮橋]] 風日祈宮は内宮の境内別宮である。祭神は[[Toyouke Daijingu|外宮（豊受大神宮）]]別宮の{{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}}と同じ[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命・級長戸辺命]]で、内宮神楽殿前から南方へ向かう参道にある'''風日祈宮橋'''（かざひのみのみやばし）で[[五十鈴川]]支流の島路川を渡った先に風日祈宮がある。 風日祈宮御橋は長さ45.6m、幅4.6mで、内宮前の[[宇治橋 (伊勢市)|宇治橋]]を小さくしたような橋である。最初にかけられたのは[[室町時代]]の[[1498年]]（[[明応]]7年）で、南端の擬宝珠（ぎぼし）に「太神宮風宮　五十鈴川御橋明応七年戊午本願観阿弥　敬白」と刻まれている。古くはこの橋がかかる川が五十鈴川本流とされており、この橋を'''五十鈴川橋'''と呼んだ。もっとも長い川が河川の本流と定義され、島路川と呼ばれてからは風日祈宮橋と呼ぶのが一般的となった。 別宮とは「'''わけみや'''」の意味で、正宮に次ぎ尊いとされる。内宮の別宮は風日祈宮のほか境内に{{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}}（あらまつりのみや）1宮、境外に[[月讀宮]]（つきよみのみや）、{{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}（たきはらのみや）、{{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}（いざわのみや）、[[倭姫宮]]（やまとひめのみや）ほか4宮があるが、風宮が別宮となったのは[[1293年]]（[[正応]]6年）で、[[1923年]]（[[大正]]12年）創建の[[倭姫宮]]の次に新しく、順位は内宮別宮10社中9位とされる。 古くは現在の末社格の'''風神社'''であったが、[[1281年]]（[[弘安]]4年）の[[元寇]]の時に[[神風]]を起こし日本を守ったとして別宮に昇格した。 == 祭神 == 風日祈宮の祭神は、風雨を司る神とされる[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命と級長戸辺命]]（しなつひこのみこと、しなとべのみこと）である。本来は農耕に適した風雨をもたらす神であったが、元寇以降は日本の国難に際して日本を救う祈願の対象となった。 == 歴史 == 風日祈宮の由緒は定かではないが、[[804年]]（[[延暦]]23年）の『皇太神宮儀式帳』の「風神社」（ふうじんのやしろ）が初出である。風神社は現在の末社に相当し、現在の摂社以下の扱いであった。 [[吾妻鏡]]に[[1187年]]（[[文治]]3年）に[[源頼朝]]が神宮に8頭の[[神馬]]を奉納したと記されている。その時の宮社の'''風'''が風神社とされるが、[[外宮]][[権禰宜]]の度会光生が取り次いでいるので外宮の風社（現在の風宮）の可能性がある。 風神社は末社相当であったが、祭神が農耕に都合のよい風雨をもたらす神であることから'''風日祈祭'''が行なわれ、[[神嘗祭]]では'''懸税'''（かけちから、稲穂）が供えられるなど重視された。 [[1281年]]（[[弘安]]4年）の[[元寇]]では[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]より[[二条為氏]]大納言が勅使として神宮に派遣され、風神社と風社で祈祷を行なった。日本に押し寄せた元軍は退却し日本にとっての国難は去り、これを'''神風'''による勝利として[[1293年]]（[[正応]]6年）に風神社と風社は別宮に昇格され、'''風日祈宮'''と'''風宮'''となった。 [[江戸時代]]末期に欧米諸国が日本を訪れるようになり、[[1863年]]（[[文久]]3年）5月に朝廷は風日祈宮と{{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}}で[[攘夷]]の祈願を15日間行なった。 1975年（昭和50年）9月5日昼過ぎ、風日祈宮の社殿瑞垣御門の扉に向けて、ビール瓶に油を詰めた[[火炎瓶]]が投げられた。油に火がつき扉は炎上したものの、その場に居合わせた人が砂利をかけて消火したため、大事には至らなかった。折から[[三重県]]で開催されていた[[国民体育大会]]・三重国体への[[皇太子]]行啓に合わせて[[過激派]]が起こした犯行と見られ、近くからは天皇訪米に反対する旨主張するビラが見つかった（[[風日祈宮放火事件]]）。 == 祭事 == 毎年5月14日と8月4日に風雨の順調と五穀の豊穣を祈る'''風日祈祭'''が行なわれる。 == 社殿 == 風日祈宮の社殿は内宮に準じ[[千木・鰹木|内削ぎの千木と、6本で偶数の鰹木]]を持つ萱葺の[[神明造]]で南面している。遷宮のための古殿地（新御敷地）は東西に隣接している。 == 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鳥羽線|鳥羽線]][[五十鈴川駅]]から約3km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]内宮前バス停下車。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢インターチェンジ]]から約2.1km。 * 駐車場：内宮前に無料駐車場があるが、正月などは利用できない。 == 参考文献 == {{No footnotes|date=2019年10月|section=1}} *矢野憲一『伊勢神宮　知られざる杜のうち』（角川選書、平成18年） *矢野憲一『伊勢神宮の衣食住』（[[角川ソフィア文庫]]、平成20年） *[[所功]]『伊勢神宮』（[[講談社学術文庫]]　平成5年） *『伊勢神宮』（[[保育社]]、平成8年）　※カラーブックス *[[櫻井勝之進]] 『伊勢神宮』（[[学生社]]、昭和44年5月、新版平成10年4月） *櫻井勝之進 『伊勢の大神の宮』（堀書店、昭和48年3月） *:※新版が『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』（[[国書刊行会]]、平成3年）にある。 == 関連項目 == * [[シナツヒコ]] * [[神風]] * [[元寇]] * [[皇大神宮]] * [[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] * [[風日祈宮放火事件]] == 外部リンク {{神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[image:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|風日祈宮]] | 名称=風日祈宮 | 所在地=三重県伊勢市宇治館町 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命
級長戸辺命]] | 社格=[[皇大神宮]]別宮 | 創建=[[804年]]以前 | 本殿=[[神明造]] | 例祭=風日祈祭 }} '''風日祈宮'''（かざひのみのみや）は[[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町にある[[皇大神宮|内宮（皇大神宮）]]の境内別宮である。 == 概要 == [[image:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|風日祈宮橋]] 風日祈宮は内宮の境内別宮である。祭神は[[Toyouke Daijingu|外宮（豊受大神宮）]]別宮の{{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}}と同じ[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命・級長戸辺命]]で、内宮神楽殿前から南方へ向かう参道にある'''風日祈宮橋'''（かざひのみのみやばし）で[[五十鈴川]]支流の島路川を渡った先に風日祈宮がある。 風日祈宮御橋は長さ45.6m、幅4.6mで、内宮前の[[宇治橋 (伊勢市)|宇治橋]]を小さくしたような橋である。最初にかけられたのは[[室町時代]]の[[1498年]]（[[明応]]7年）で、南端の擬宝珠（ぎぼし）に「太神宮風宮　五十鈴川御橋明応七年戊午本願観阿弥　敬白」と刻まれている。古くはこの橋がかかる川が五十鈴川本流とされており、この橋を'''五十鈴川橋'''と呼んだ。もっとも長い川が河川の本流と定義され、島路川と呼ばれてからは風日祈宮橋と呼ぶのが一般的となった。 別宮とは「'''わけみや'''」の意味で、正宮に次ぎ尊いとされる。内宮の別宮は風日祈宮のほか境内に{{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}}（あらまつりのみや）1宮、境外に{{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}（つきよみのみや）、{{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}（たきはらのみや）、{{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}（いざわのみや）、{{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}（やまとひめのみや）ほか4宮があるが、風宮が別宮となったのは[[1293年]]（[[正応]]6年）で、[[1923年]]（[[大正]]12年）創建の{{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}の次に新しく、順位は内宮別宮10社中9位とされる。 古くは現在の末社格の'''風神社'''であったが、[[1281年]]（[[弘安]]4年）の[[元寇]]の時に[[神風]]を起こし日本を守ったとして別宮に昇格した。 == 祭神 == 風日祈宮の祭神は、風雨を司る神とされる[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命と級長戸辺命]]（しなつひこのみこと、しなとべのみこと）である。本来は農耕に適した風雨をもたらす神であったが、元寇以降は日本の国難に際して日本を救う祈願の対象となった。 == 歴史 == 風日祈宮の由緒は定かではないが、[[804年]]（[[延暦]]23年）の『皇太神宮儀式帳』の「風神社」（ふうじんのやしろ）が初出である。風神社は現在の末社に相当し、現在の摂社以下の扱いであった。 [[吾妻鏡]]に[[1187年]]（[[文治]]3年）に[[源頼朝]]が神宮に8頭の[[神馬]]を奉納したと記されている。その時の宮社の'''風'''が風神社とされるが、[[外宮]][[権禰宜]]の度会光生が取り次いでいるので外宮の風社（現在の風宮）の可能性がある。 風神社は末社相当であったが、祭神が農耕に都合のよい風雨をもたらす神であることから'''風日祈祭'''が行なわれ、[[神嘗祭]]では'''懸税'''（かけちから、稲穂）が供えられるなど重視された。 [[1281年]]（[[弘安]]4年）の[[元寇]]では[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]より[[二条為氏]]大納言が勅使として神宮に派遣され、風神社と風社で祈祷を行なった。日本に押し寄せた元軍は退却し日本にとっての国難は去り、これを'''神風'''による勝利として[[1293年]]（[[正応]]6年）に風神社と風社は別宮に昇格され、'''風日祈宮'''と'''風宮'''となった。 [[江戸時代]]末期に欧米諸国が日本を訪れるようになり、[[1863年]]（[[文久]]3年）5月に朝廷は風日祈宮と{{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}}で[[攘夷]]の祈願を15日間行なった。 1975年（昭和50年）9月5日昼過ぎ、風日祈宮の社殿瑞垣御門の扉に向けて、ビール瓶に油を詰めた[[火炎瓶]]が投げられた。油に火がつき扉は炎上したものの、その場に居合わせた人が砂利をかけて消火したため、大事には至らなかった。折から[[三重県]]で開催されていた[[国民体育大会]]・三重国体への[[皇太子]]行啓に合わせて[[過激派]]が起こした犯行と見られ、近くからは天皇訪米に反対する旨主張するビラが見つかった（[[風日祈宮放火事件]]）。 == 祭事 == 毎年5月14日と8月4日に風雨の順調と五穀の豊穣を祈る'''風日祈祭'''が行なわれる。 == 社殿 == 風日祈宮の社殿は内宮に準じ[[千木・鰹木|内削ぎの千木と、6本で偶数の鰹木]]を持つ萱葺の[[神明造]]で南面している。遷宮のための古殿地（新御敷地）は東西に隣接している。 == 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鳥羽線|鳥羽線]][[五十鈴川駅]]から約3km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]内宮前バス停下車。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢インターチェンジ]]から約2.1km。 * 駐車場：内宮前に無料駐車場があるが、正月などは利用できない。 == 参考文献 == {{No footnotes|date=2019年10月|section=1}} *矢野憲一『伊勢神宮　知られざる杜のうち』（角川選書、平成18年） *矢野憲一『伊勢神宮の衣食住』（[[角川ソフィア文庫]]、平成20年） *[[所功]]『伊勢神宮』（[[講談社学術文庫]]　平成5年） *『伊勢神宮』（[[保育社]]、平成8年）　※カラーブックス *[[櫻井勝之進]] 『伊勢神宮』（[[学生社]]、昭和44年5月、新版平成10年4月） *櫻井勝之進 『伊勢の大神の宮』（堀書店、昭和48年3月） *:※新版が『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』（[[国書刊行会]]、平成3年）にある。 == 関連項目 == * [[シナツヒコ]] * [[神風]] * [[元寇]] * [[皇大神宮]] * [[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] * [[風日祈宮放火事件]] == 外部リンク == Kazahinomi-no-Miya == {{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} Kazahinomi-no-Miya (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River (Mie)|Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the [[Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident]]. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kotaijingu]] * [[List of Shrines and Facilities at Ise Jingu]] * [[Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident]] {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{神道 横}} {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:untranslated pages]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} Kazahinomi-no-Miya (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River (Mie)|Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kotaijingu]] * [[List of Shrines and Facilities at Ise Jingu]] * [[Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident]] {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{神道 横}} {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:untranslated pages]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]]

The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * [[List of Shrines and Facilities at Ise Jingu]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{神道 横}} {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:untranslated pages]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} Kazahinomi-no-Miya (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River (Mie)|Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * [[List of Shrines and Facilities at Ise Jingu]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{神道 横}} {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:untranslated pages]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} Kazahinomi-no-Miya (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:untranslated pages]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:qq]]

In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kazabi-no-Miya]][[en:Kazabi-no-Miya]]

It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Kazahinomi-no-Miya-qq]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kazahinomi-no-Miya-qq]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kazahinomi-no-Miya-qq]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kazahinomi-no-Miya-qq]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kazahinomi-no-Miya-qq]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise|かさひのみのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kazahinomi-no-Miya-qq]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[ja:風日祈宮]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]
