It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|風日祈宮|version=97915581|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] }} [[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[ja:風日祈宮]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izawa-no-miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-Miya|qq=}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Shikinen Sengu|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|風日祈宮|version=97915581|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] }} [[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[ja:風日祈宮]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_Rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izawa-no-miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-Miya|qq=}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izawa-no-miya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-Miya|qq=}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Jingū Shikinen Sengū|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|風日祈宮|version=97915581|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[ja:風日祈宮]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_Rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise City)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-miya]], and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-Miya|qq=}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Jingū Shikinen Sengū|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|風日祈宮|version=97915581|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[ja:風日祈宮]] [[en:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] {{Commons category|Kazahinomi-no-miya}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_Rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise City)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Jingū Shikinen Sengū|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|風日祈宮|version=97915581|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[ja:風日祈宮]] {{Commons category|Kazahinomi-no-miya}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_Rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise City)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Jingū Shikinen Sengū|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[:en:Kamikaze|Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|風日祈宮|version=97915581|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[ja:風日祈宮]] {{Commons category|Kazahinomi-no-miya}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_Rank=[[Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise City)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-miya]], and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-Miya|qq=}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-miya]], and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-Miya|qq=}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Jingū Shikinen Sengū|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[:en:Kamikaze|Kamikaze]] * [[Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|風日祈宮|version=97915581|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[ja:風日祈宮]] {{Commons category|Kazahinomi-no-miya}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_Rank=[[:en:Kotaijingu|Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[:en:Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise City)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-miya]], and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-Miya|qq=}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. In ancient times, it was known as "Kazasha Shrine" and had the same Rank as the current massha (末社, "subordinate shrines"). However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. Originally, they were considered gods of favorable weather for agriculture, but after the Mongol invasions, they became associated with divine intervention in national crises. == History == The origins of Kazahinomi-no-Miya are unclear, but it first appears as "Kazasha Shrine" in the 804 CE (Enryaku 23) text *Kotaijingu Gishikicho*. Initially, it held a status equivalent to a massha. The *Azuma Kagami* records that in 1187 (Bunji 3), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] dedicated eight sacred horses to the shrines. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6). In the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], as Japan faced increasing foreign pressure, the imperial court conducted 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners (*jōi*) at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]] in 1863 (Bunkyū 3). On September 5, 1975, a firebomb attack was attempted at Kazahinomi-no-Miya. An incendiary device made from a beer bottle filled with oil was thrown at the shrine’s inner gate, setting it ablaze. Fortunately, a visitor quickly extinguished the fire with gravel, preventing major damage. The attack was believed to have been carried out by radicals protesting the Emperor’s planned visit to the United States. Pamphlets opposing the visit were found at the scene. This incident became known as the {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}}. == Rituals == The annual *Kazahinomi-sai* is held on May 14 and August 4, praying for favorable weather and a bountiful harvest. == Shrine Buildings == Kazahinomi-no-Miya’s shrine buildings follow the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style, with chigi cut in the inward style (*uchisogi*) and an even number of six *katsuogi*. The roofs are thatched. The former shrine grounds (*shingoshikichi*) are located adjacent to the east and west to accommodate periodic reconstructions during the [[Jingū Shikinen Sengū|Shikinen Sengu]] ritual. == Access == * Nearest train station: [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Toba Line|Toba Line]] [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], approximately 3 km away. * Nearest bus stop: [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Naiku-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest highway exit: [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] [[Ise Interchange]], approximately 2.1 km away. * Parking: Free parking is available near Naiku, though it may be restricted during New Year's and special events. == See Also == * [[Shinatsuhiko]] * [[:en:Kamikaze|Kamikaze]] * [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] * [[Kōtai Jingū|Kotaijingu]] * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt_ja=125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=風日祈宮放火事件|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Arson Incident|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=}} {{commonscat|Kazahinomi-no-miya}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/naigu/naigu7.htm 伊勢の神宮] {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|風日祈宮|version=97915581|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] [[ja:風日祈宮]] {{Commons category|Kazahinomi-no-miya}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image=[[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 01.JPG|256px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya]] | name=Kazahinomi-no-Miya | location=Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture | coordinates={{Coord|34|27|15|N|136|43|21|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deities=[[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]]
[[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]] | shrine_Rank=[[:en:Kotaijingu|Kotaijingu]] Betsugu | established=Before 804 CE | main_building=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | annual_festival=Kazahinomi-sai }} '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya''' (風日祈宮) is a shrine located within the precincts of [[:en:Kotaijingu|Naiku (Kotaijingu)]] in Ujikatamachi, Ise, Mie Prefecture. == Overview == [[File:Kazahinomi-no-miya 02.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge]] Kazahinomi-no-Miya is one of the Betsugu (associated shrines) of Naiku. It enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], the same deities venerated at [[Kaze-no-Miya]] of [[Toyouke Daijingu|Geku (Toyouke Daijingu)]]. It is reached by crossing the '''Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge''', which spans the Shimaji River, a tributary of the [[Isuzu River]]. Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge is 45.6 meters long and 4.6 meters wide, resembling a smaller version of [[Uji Bridge (Ise City)|Uji Bridge]] at the entrance of Naiku. The bridge was first built in 1498 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], as evidenced by an inscription on the southern giboshi (decorative finial) that reads, "Dai Jingu Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Isuzu River Bridge, built in Meiō 7 (戊午年), dedicated by Kanami, reverently stated." In ancient times, the river under this bridge was considered the main stream of the Isuzu River and was referred to as the "Isuzu River Bridge." However, when it was later identified as the tributary Shimaji River, the bridge became commonly known as Kazahinomi-no-Miya Bridge. A Betsugu (別宮, "separate shrine") is a secondary shrine considered highly important, second only to the main sanctuary. In addition to Kazahinomi-no-Miya, Naiku has one other Betsugu within its precincts, {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|fr|Aramatsuri_no_miya|es|Aramatsuri no miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}} (Aramatsuri-no-Miya). Outside Naiku’s precincts, there are additional Betsugu, including {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|ja|月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-miya]], and {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Miya|ja|倭姫宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-Miya|qq=}}. Kazahinomi-no-Miya was originally considered a subordinate shrine and was only elevated to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6), making it the ninth in Rank among Naiku's ten Betsugu. However, during the 1281 Mongol invasions, the deities were believed to have summoned the [[Divine Wind (kamikaze)|kamikaze]] that protected Japan, leading to its elevation to Betsugu status. == Enshrined Deities == Kazahinomi-no-Miya enshrines [[Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko-no-Mikoto]] and [[Shinatobe-no-Mikoto]], deities of wind and rain. It mentions an offering at "Kazasha Shrine," which is believed to be this shrine, though some scholars suggest it might refer to the shrine at Geku (now [[Kaze-no-Miya]]). Despite its lower Rank, Kazahinomi-no-Miya was still highly revered. The *Kazahinomi-sai* festival was held to pray for favorable winds and rain for agriculture, and during the [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]], offerings of rice ears (kakechikara) were made to the shrine. During the 1281 Mongol invasions (*Genkō* 4), the imperial court sent a special envoy, Dainagon [[:en:Nijō Tameuji|Nijō Tameuji]], to perform rituals at Kazahinomi-no-Miya and [[Kaze-no-Miya]]. The subsequent storm that destroyed the Mongol fleet was attributed to divine intervention, leading to the shrine’s elevation to Betsugu status in 1293 (Shōō 6).
